w:r.w:t: Configuration | JPA | Service Layer | REST API | Exception Handling | Storage Patterns
12 Exam-Style Problems with Full Solutions & Line-by-Line Explanations
Each problem below simulates an actual lab quiz question from CSE 2218. The document is structured in three parts per problem:
The exam prompt exactly as your faculty would phrase it
Read it, close the solution, try to code it yourself first
Full working code that would pass the JUnit tests
Compare your attempt line by line
Why each annotation and decision was made
Read this even if you got it right — the why matters
Attempt each problem yourself before reading the solution. Even a partial attempt trains your muscle memory for the exam.
This document covers four data storage approaches you might encounter:
Standard quiz setup — data resets on restart
Real DB variant — same JPA code, diff properties
Simpler variant without JPA, uses ArrayList
You are given a blank Spring Boot project with spring-boot-starter-data-jpa and the H2 dependency.
a) The database runs in memory (data is lost on restart)
b) Hibernate creates / updates tables automatically
c) SQL statements are printed to the console
d) The H2 browser console is accessible at /h2-console
-> 'mem:' means in-memory. 'testdb' is just the database name. The data vanishes when the app stops.
-> Tells Spring which JDBC driver to use. Required even though Spring can auto-detect it — be explicit.
-> H2's default credentials. 'sa' stands for System Administrator. Password is an empty string, NOT 'null'.
-> 'update' creates missing tables/columns on startup but NEVER drops existing data.
-> Safe for development. NEVER use 'create' or 'create-drop' in production.
-> Every INSERT / SELECT / UPDATE Hibernate executes is printed to the console.
-> Invaluable for debugging N+1 problems and unexpected queries.
-> Opens a web-based SQL client at http://localhost:8080/h2-console
-> Use the same JDBC URL (jdbc:h2:mem:testdb) to log in and inspect the database.
QUESTION 2 | H2 File-Based vs MySQL Configuration
Your team needs to switch from an in-memory H2 database to:
Variant A: H2 File-Based (data persists between restarts, still no separate DB server)
Variant B: MySQL running on localhost:3306, database name 'school', user 'root', password 'root'
The JPA and entity code does NOT change — only the properties file.
-> The './data/schooldb' is a relative file path. Spring creates schooldb.mv.db in the data/ folder.
-> Data survives app restarts because it is written to disk, not RAM.
-> H2 console still works — use the same file URL to connect.
-> You MUST create the database (schema) first: CREATE DATABASE school;
-> Driver is 'com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver' (the 'cj' version — legacy 'com.mysql.jdbc.Driver' is deprecated).
-> Add mysql-connector-j to pom.xml: groupId com.mysql, artifactId mysql-connector-j
KEY INSIGHT: Your @Entity, @Repository, and @Service code is IDENTICAL for all three storage types.
-> Spring Data JPA abstracts the database. Only application.properties changes.
-> This is the power of the Repository pattern — you swap databases by changing config, not code.
QUESTION 3 | Create a JPA Entity with Constraints
Create a Student entity mapped to a table named 'students' with these requirements:
- 'studentId' column: unique, NOT NULL (stores the university student number)
- Provide a no-arg constructor AND a parameterised constructor
private String major; // nullable by default
public Student(String name, String studentId, Double gpa, String major) {
public void setName(String n) { this.name = n; }
public void setStudentId(String s) { this.studentId = s; }
public void setGpa(Double g) { this.gpa = g; }
public void setMajor(String m) { this.major = m; }
@Entity -> Tells Hibernate: 'manage this class as a database table'.
@Table(name="students") -> Without this, Hibernate uses the class name ('Student' -> table 'student').
Always use explicit @Table so your DB schema is predictable.
-> IDENTITY means the database auto-increments the column (MySQL AUTO_INCREMENT, H2/Postgres SERIAL).
-> The Long type is required — primitive 'long' cannot hold null (important for new unsaved entities).
-> nullable=false adds a NOT NULL constraint at the DB level.
-> length=80 sets VARCHAR(80). Default is VARCHAR(255).
@Column(unique=true) -> Adds a UNIQUE index. Perfect for student IDs, email addresses, etc.
-> precision = total significant digits, scale = digits after decimal.
-> precision=4, scale=2 stores values like 3.75 (99.99 max, 0.01 min).
NO ANNOTATION on 'major' -> Spring uses the field name as the column name. Column is nullable by default.
1. no-arg constructor — JPA uses reflection to create instances when loading from DB.
2. parameterised constructor — makes it easy to create new entities in your service/seeder.
QUESTION 4 | Spring Data Repository with Custom Query Methods
Given the Student entity from Question 3, create a StudentRepository that:
a) Extends JpaRepository with the correct type parameters
b) Adds a method to find all students by major
c) Adds a method to find a student by their studentId field
d) Adds a method to find students whose GPA is >= a given value
e) Adds a custom @Query to search by name (case-insensitive, partial match)
public interface StudentRepository extends JpaRepository<Student, Long> {
// (b) Find all by major — Spring generates: SELECT * FROM students WHERE major = ?
// (c) Find by studentId — returns Optional because it may not exist
// (d) GPA >= threshold — Spring reads 'GreaterThanEqual' from method name
// (e) Custom JPQL — case-insensitive partial name search
@Query("SELECT s FROM Student s WHERE LOWER(s.name) LIKE LOWER(CONCAT('%', :name, '%'))")
// BONUS: Combined condition (Spring generates the AND automatically)
-> First type = the entity class. Second type = the type of the @Id field (Long).
-> You inherit: save(), findById(), findAll(), deleteById(), existsById(), count() for FREE.
Spring reads the method name and generates SQL — you write ZERO SQL for these.
-> Use Optional when you expect 0 or 1 results (findByStudentId — student IDs are unique).
-> Use List when you expect 0 or many results (findByMajor — many students can share a major).
-> JPQL uses CLASS names (Student) not TABLE names (students).
-> CONCAT('%', :name, '%') builds the LIKE pattern dynamically.
-> @Param('name') links the method parameter to the :name placeholder in the query.
-> LOWER() on both sides makes the search case-insensitive.
QUESTION 5 | Data Seeding — CommandLineRunner with Student ID
b) Uses constructor injection to get the StudentRepository
c) Seeds exactly 3 students on app startup
d) One of the students MUST have 'STU20240001' as their studentId (the quiz anti-cheat requirement)
e) Prints a confirmation message after seeding
f) Does NOT seed duplicates if the app restarts (check before inserting)
// Constructor injection — single constructor, @Autowired not required
public void run(String... args) throws Exception {
// (f) Guard: only seed if the table is empty
System.out.println("[Seeder] Database already contains data — skipping.");
// (c,d) Seed 3 students — one MUST match the quiz's required studentId
CommandLineRunner.run() is called by Spring AFTER the entire application context is ready.
-> This means all beans are created, all repositories are connected to the DB, and tables exist.
-> With ddl-auto=update, tables are NOT dropped on restart.
-> Without the guard, every restart would try to insert the same records.
-> repo.count() returns the number of rows in the table — if > 0, skip seeding.
WHY CONSTRUCTOR INJECTION in the Seeder?
-> @Component classes are Spring-managed beans, so they support all injection types.
-> Single constructor -> @Autowired is optional but the injection still works.
-> DataSeeder depends on StudentRepository, so it is injected at construction time.
-> Your faculty's JUnit tests look for a specific studentId in the seeded data.
-> If your seeder does not insert the correct studentId, the test fails even if everything else works.
-> Always re-read the quiz prompt to find the required seed value.
QUESTION 6 | Full Service Layer with Exception Throwing
Implement StudentService with the following methods:
b) getStudentById(Long id) — throws StudentNotFoundException if not found
c) getStudentByStudentId(String studentId) — throws StudentNotFoundException if not found
d) createStudent(Student student) — saves and returns the student
e) updateStudent(Long id, Student updatedData) — updates name/gpa/major, throws if not found
f) deleteStudent(Long id) — throws if not found, then deletes
Also write the custom StudentNotFoundException class.
super("Student not found with id: " + id);
public Student updateStudent(Long id, Student updatedData) {
Student existing = getStudentById(id); // reuse (b) — throws if missing
return repo.save(existing); // save() = UPDATE if id exists
-> RuntimeException = unchecked: callers (controllers) do NOT need try-catch.
-> The @RestControllerAdvice will catch it globally and return the right HTTP status.
-> If you used 'extends Exception' (checked), every caller would need 'throws' or try-catch — messy.
-> repo.findById() returns Optional<Student> — it NEVER returns null.
-> If the Optional is empty (no row with that id), the lambda runs and throws the exception.
-> The lambda is only evaluated if the Optional IS empty — no wasted object creation.
-> Step 1: call getStudentById() — this throws if the student doesn't exist (reuse logic).
-> Step 2: update ONLY the fields you allow the client to change (NOT id, NOT studentId).
-> Step 3: repo.save(existing) — save() does UPDATE because the entity already has an id.
-> Never trust the client's id in the request body — always use the URL path variable id.
-> repo.deleteById() throws EmptyResultDataAccessException if id not found (Spring 2.x).
-> Explicitly checking with existsById() gives you control over the error message.
-> With Spring 3.x, deleteById() silently does nothing if not found — checking is even more important.
QUESTION 7 | Service Without a Database — In-Memory List Storage
Some quiz variants do NOT use JPA at all. Instead, store data in an ArrayList.
a) Stores Product objects in a private ArrayList (no @Repository, no database)
b) Auto-increments an ID for each new product
c) Supports: getAll(), getById(int id), create(Product p), delete(int id)
d) Throws ProductNotFoundException when a product is not found
Product has: int id, String name, double price
public Product(int id, String name, double price) {
this.id = id; this.name = name; this.price = price;
public void setId(int id) { this.id = id; }
public void setName(String n) { this.name = n; }
public void setPrice(double p) { this.price = p; }
// In-memory store — singleton bean means this list lives for the app lifetime
private final List<Product> store = new ArrayList<>();
private final AtomicInteger idCounter = new AtomicInteger(1);
Product p = getById(id); // throws if not found
-> Quiz variants that say 'no database required' or don't include JPA dependencies.
-> Useful for understanding the service pattern without DB complexity.
-> In real projects, this is used for caches, feature flags, or lightweight lookups.
-> @Service beans are singletons by default — one instance for the entire application.
-> The ArrayList lives inside that singleton, so it persists for the app's lifetime.
-> Data is LOST when the app restarts — it is purely in-memory.
-> Provides thread-safe auto-increment without needing a database sequence.
-> getAndIncrement() returns the current value then increments — similar to AUTO_INCREMENT.
-> Regular int++ is not thread-safe if multiple requests arrive simultaneously.
-> Replaces a for-loop with functional Java 8+ stream API.
-> filter(p -> p.getId() == id) keeps only the matching product.
-> orElseThrow() throws if the Optional is empty (product not in list).
QUESTION 8 | Full CRUD REST Controller with Student ID URL Prefix
Implement StudentController for the Student entity. Requirements:
a) Base URL must be: /api/{studentId}/students (where {studentId} is the URL prefix from YOUR student number)
c) GET /api/{studentId}/students/{id} -> 200 OK + one student, or 404 if not found
e) PUT /api/{studentId}/students/{id} -> 200 OK + updated student, or 404
g) POST must return a custom Location header pointing to the new resource
// (c) GET one student by DB id
// (d,g) POST — create and return 201 + Location header
@PathVariable String studentId, // the URL prefix variable
-> The {studentId} in the PATH is a path variable for the URL prefix (your student number).
-> It is NOT the same as the student's database id — the naming is just a coincidence in this quiz.
-> The @PathVariable String studentId in the create() method captures this URL segment.
-> .created(URI) sets status 201 AND automatically adds a Location header.
-> Location header tells the client WHERE the new resource can be found.
-> This is HTTP best practice for POST endpoints — the JUnit test may check for this header.
-> .noContent() sets status 204. .build() creates the ResponseEntity with NO body.
-> DELETE should return 204, not 200. 200 means 'here is the deleted thing' which is odd.
-> Spring automatically converts the URL segment to the parameter type.
EXAM TRAP — TWO PATH VARIABLES WITH THE SAME NAME:
-> In the delete/update methods you only declare @PathVariable Long id — not both.
-> You only need to declare the ones you USE in the method. Spring handles the rest.
QUESTION 9 | Global Exception Handler with Structured Error Response
c) Catches any other Exception -> 500 Internal Server Error
d) Returns a structured JSON error body with: status (int), message (String), timestamp (String)
e) Also create the ErrorResponse DTO class used as the return body
// Getters — Jackson needs these to serialize to JSON
// (c) 500 — anything else (keep this last — most specific handlers win)
-> A special @Component that intercepts exceptions thrown by ANY @RestController.
-> = @ControllerAdvice + @ResponseBody (so return values are auto-serialized to JSON).
-> You only need ONE per application — it covers all controllers globally.
-> Spring picks the MOST SPECIFIC matching handler.
-> StudentNotFoundException is more specific than Exception — so it always wins for that type.
-> The @ExceptionHandler(Exception.class) is the catch-all fallback for anything else.
ErrorResponse DTO — WHY NOT just return a String?
-> A String body returns plain text. A DTO returns structured JSON:
-> JUnit tests often deserialize the response body and check specific fields.
-> Jackson (the JSON library bundled with Spring Boot) automatically converts the DTO to JSON.
-> Jackson requires GETTERS — fields alone are not enough for serialization.
-> Spring returns a 500 JSON with Hibernate/Spring internals in the message.
-> The JUnit test expects a 404 but gets 500 — test FAILS.
-> Always write the global handler — it is required for correct status codes.
QUESTION 10 | Complete Mini-App — Course Management (Exam Simulation)
Build a complete Spring Boot mini-app for managing university courses.
Entity: Course (id: Long auto-gen, code: String unique+notNull, title: String notNull, credits: int, instructor: String)
- Seed 3 courses on startup (one must have code='CSE2218')
public Course(String code, String title, int credits, String instructor) {
public void setCode(String c) { this.code = c; }
public void setTitle(String t) { this.title = t; }
public void setCredits(int c) { this.credits = c; }
public void setInstructor(String i) { this.instructor = i; }
public interface CourseRepository extends JpaRepository<Course, Long> {
public Course create(Course c) { return repo.save(c); }
throw new CourseNotFoundException("Course not found with id: " + id);
if (repo.count() > 0) return; // skip if already seeded
This problem simulates the ACTUAL exam format end-to-end. Notice these patterns:
FILE STRUCTURE (every Spring Boot project follows this):
GlobalExceptionHandler -> @RestControllerAdvice, maps exceptions to HTTP codes
INJECTION CHAIN: Controller <- Service <- Repository <- Database
-> Each layer injects the one below it via constructor injection.
-> You never skip a layer (Controller never calls Repository directly).
1. Does your entity have a no-arg constructor?
2. Does your seeder use the required studentId / data value?
6. Does @RestControllerAdvice return 404 for your custom exception?
QUESTION 11 | DTO Pattern — Separating Request, Response and Entity
Refactor the Student entity to use proper DTOs:
a) StudentRequest DTO — fields: name, studentId, gpa, major (no id field)
b) StudentResponse DTO — fields: id, name, studentId, major (no gpa — pretend it is sensitive)
c) Rewrite the POST and GET endpoints to accept/return DTOs instead of the entity directly
d) Write the conversion logic (entity -> response, request -> entity)
public void setName(String n) { this.name = n; }
public void setStudentId(String s) { this.studentId = s; }
public void setGpa(Double g) { this.gpa = g; }
public void setMajor(String m) { this.major = m; }
// Note: NO gpa field — intentionally hidden
public StudentResponse(Long id, String name, String studentId, String major) {
// Convert Entity -> Response DTO (for returning to client)
@RequestBody StudentRequest request) { // <-- takes DTO not entity
-> SECURITY: Prevent 'mass assignment' attacks. If Entity has a 'role' field and you accept
the entity directly in @RequestBody, a malicious client could set role='ADMIN'.
-> SEPARATION: Your API contract (what fields are exposed) is independent of your DB schema.
-> EVOLUTION: You can rename DB columns without changing the API, and vice versa.
-> The client should NEVER send an id when creating a resource.
-> The id is assigned by the database (auto-increment) — the client has no say.
-> If you accept the entity directly and the client sends id=1, they might accidentally overwrite data.
-> The gpa field is intentionally missing from StudentResponse.
-> Jackson only serializes fields that have getters in the returned object.
-> No getter in StudentResponse -> no gpa in the JSON response.
-> In simple projects, write these as private methods in the Service.
-> In larger projects, use a dedicated Mapper class or MapStruct library.
-> For the exam, inline conversion in the service is perfectly fine.
QUESTION 12 | Tricky Bugs — Spot and Fix the Errors
Each code snippet below contains a common mistake. Identify the bug and write the corrected version.
Bug 1: The POST endpoint returns 200 instead of 201.
Bug 2: The service crashes with NullPointerException instead of 404.
Bug 3: The entity fails to load from the DB with 'No default constructor found'.
Bug 4: The seeder inserts duplicates every time the app restarts.
Bug 5: The exception handler is never triggered — Spring still returns 500.
// FIXED — orElseThrow gives a meaningful exception the handler can catch:
// BUGGY — only has a parameterised constructor, JPA cannot instantiate:
// FIXED — always add a no-arg constructor alongside parameterised ones:
// BUGGY — inserts 3 students EVERY restart because there is no guard:
// FIXED — check count() first; skip if data already exists:
// The exception IS caught but the String return value is treated as a VIEW NAME, not JSON.
Bug 1 — .ok() always returns 200. POST must return 201 (CREATED). The JUnit test checks the status code.
Bug 2 — .get() on an empty Optional throws NoSuchElementException (not NotFoundException).
-> NoSuchElementException falls through to the generic handler -> 500 instead of 404.
-> orElseThrow() guarantees YOUR exception is thrown with YOUR message.
Bug 3 — JPA uses reflection to call a no-arg constructor when mapping rows from the DB to Java objects.
-> If you define ANY constructor, Java no longer auto-generates the no-arg one.
-> You MUST explicitly write public Student() {}
Bug 4 — With ddl-auto=update, H2 file-based and any real DB keep data between restarts.
-> Without the guard, running the app 5 times inserts 10 students (5 * 2).
-> repo.count() > 0 is the simplest guard. More robust: check for a specific record.
-> @ControllerAdvice is for MVC controllers that return VIEW NAMES (HTML templates).
-> It does not automatically add @ResponseBody to handler methods.
-> @RestControllerAdvice adds @ResponseBody, so return values are serialized to JSON.
-> Always use @RestControllerAdvice for REST API exception handlers.
Quick Reference — Exam Day Cheat Sheet
Client sent invalid data / validation failed
1. Does every @Entity have a no-arg constructor?
2. Does the seeder use the exact studentId the quiz requires?
4. Does POST return 201? Does DELETE return 204?
5. Does @RestControllerAdvice return 404 for your custom exception?
6. Are all dependencies using constructor injection (private final)?
7. Does application.properties have the correct datasource URL and ddl-auto?
